coredata.dc:title: “On/off/on” hydrogen-peroxide sensor with hemoglobin-functionalized carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Bhunia, Susanta Kumar
Dolai, Susmita
Sun, Hongchen
Jelinek, Raz
coredata.dc:description: We demonstrate a new scheme for hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) detection through a distinctive fluorescence “on/off/on” mechanism. Specifically, we show that the fluorescence emission of carbon dots (C-dots) prepared from L-penicillamine (LPA) as the carbon precursor underwent initial quenching upon addition of hemoglobin (“off” state) but could be completely recovered upon addition of H2O2 (fluorescence “on”). The “on/off/on” phenomenon was observed only in case of C-dots prepared from penicillamine, underscoring a unique radical-scavenging mechanism by thiol residues upon the C-dots' surface. This study illustrates a novel C-dot fluorescence tuning through heme oxidation and demonstrates a useful sensing modality for oxidative targets.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hydrogen peroxide sensor
Carbon dots
Fluorescence quenching
Hemoglobin
π − π Interactions

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of citric acid derived C-dots (CA-C-dots)

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Israel Ministry of Science and Technology

Planning and Budgeting Committee of the Council for Higher Education of Israel

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Dr. Susanta Kumar Bhunia is grateful to the Planning and Budgeting Committee (PBC) of the Israeli Council for Higher Education for an Outstanding Postdoctoral Fellowship. The Israel Ministry of Science and Technology is acknowledged for financial support through a China-Israel collaborative grant no. 23752-3 . Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence “on/off/on” of carbon dots prepared from L-penicillamine (LPA). A. Schematic illustration of the synthesis procedure; B. Pictures of the fluorescence “on-off-on” detection process (365 nm excitation).

Microscopic and spectroscopic characterization of the LPA-C-dots. A. High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) image showing the crystalline graphitic core. B. Excitation-dependent emission spectra. C. X ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis. The assignments of peaks to specific atomic species and surface functional groups are indicated in the spectra.

Fluorescence modulation upon interactions of Hb and H2O2 with C-dots. A. Fluorescence quenching of LPA-C-dots after incubation with different concentrations of Hb. B. Fluorescence recovery of the C-dots/Hb complex upon addition of different amounts of H2O2. (For interpretation of the references to colour in text, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

UV–vis and circular dichroism (CD) spectroscopic analyses of the interactions of Hb and H2O2 with the LPA-C-dots. A. UV–vis absorbance spectra of Hb, LPA-C-dots, and Hb-LPA-C-dots prior to and after addition of H2O2. B. CD spectra of Hb and Hb-LPA-C-dots prior to and after addition of H2O2. (For interpretation of the references to colour in text, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Fluorescence quenching and de-quenching in hemin-carbon dots. A. Fluorescence quenching of LPA-C-dots in presence of hemin (dotted curve). B. Fluorescence recovery induced upon addition of H2O2 to the hemin-C-dot complexes.
Proposed mechanism for H2O2 sensing via C-dots' fluorescence “on-off-on”.
Scavenging of hydroxyl radicals by thiol residues in LPA-C-dots. A. Scavenging mechanism of hydroxyl radicals by LPA-C-dots [61]. B. FTIR spectra of LPA-C-dots (i) and Hb-LPA-C-dots + H2O2 (ii).

head.ce:title: “On/off/on” hydrogen-peroxide sensor with hemoglobin-functionalized carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Ben Gurion University of the Negev, Beer Sheva 8410501, Israel

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Ben Gurion University of the Negev

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jilin Provincial Key Laboratory of Tooth Development and Bone Remodeling, Jilin University, Changchun 130021, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jilin Provincial Key Laboratory of Tooth Development and Bone Remodeling

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Ilse Katz Institute for Nanotechnology, Ben Gurion University of the Negev, Beer Sheva 8410501, Israel

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Ben Gurion University of the Negev

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemistry, Ben Gurion University of the Negev, Beer Sheva 8410501, Israel.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Present address: Schulich Faculty of Chemistry, Technion-Israel Institute of Technology, Haifa 3200003, Israel.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We demonstrate a new scheme for hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) detection through a distinctive fluorescence “on/off/on” mechanism. Specifically, we show that the fluorescence emission of carbon dots (C-dots) prepared from L-penicillamine (LPA) as the carbon precursor underwent initial quenching upon addition of hemoglobin (“off” state) but could be completely recovered upon addition of H2O2 (fluorescence “on”). The “on/off/on” phenomenon was observed only in case of C-dots prepared from penicillamine, underscoring a unique radical-scavenging mechanism by thiol residues upon the C-dots' surface. This study illustrates a novel C-dot fluorescence tuning through heme oxidation and demonstrates a useful sensing modality for oxidative targets.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) is the most common reactive oxygen species (ROS) [1]. H2O2 also plays an essential role as an oxidizing, bleaching, and sterilizing agent in chemical industries [2]. High concentrations of H2O2 might have adverse health effects, associated with cardiovascular disease, Alzheimer’s disease, and cancer [3–6]. Detection of H2O2 in different environments and using simple means is thus highly desirable [6,7]. Varied H2O2 detection strategies have been reported, including electrolytic analysis [8–16], high-performance liquid chromatography [17], titrimetric determination [18], and spectrophotometry [19,20]. Many of those methods, however, require expensive instrumentation, complex synthesis schemes, long detection times, and/or they exhibit insufficient sensitivity. While fluorescence-based techniques for H2O2 detection exhibit rapid response and high sensitivity [21–24], most such sensors consist of non-biocompatible or toxic semiconductor quantum dots and/or organic dyes.

Carbon dots (C-dots) constitute a powerful and versatile fluorescence sensing platform. C-dots, quasi-spherical carbonaceous nanoparticles (<10 nm), exhibit unique optical properties, good fluorescence quantum yield, water solubility, biocompatibility, and eco-friendliness [25–28]. C-dots have been widely used in recent years in bio-imaging, cell-labeling, and diverse sensing applications [29–32]. Importantly, most reported C-dot-based detection strategies have either relied upon coupling of specific recognition elements onto synthesized C-dots [33–35], or surface modification of C-dots, following which binding of the target analyte induced quenching of the C-dots' fluorescence [32,36,37]. However, an alternative C-dot sensing strategy is to exploit the “structural memory” of the C-dots' carbonaceous precursors; specifically, due to the mild reaction conditions (particularly low carbonization temperatures), C-dots, in many instances, can retain the structural and functional units of the carbonaceous building blocks. This feature effectively enables sensing modalities of specific molecular targets through the use of recognition elements and functional units of the precursor molecules [25,32,34].

H2O2 sensing by functionalized C-dots has been reported [38–45]. Wu et al. [38] functionalized C-dots with H2O2 recognition molecules for ratiometric fluorescent sensing. Zheng et al. [39] performed peroxidase-like catalytic activity of C-dots to achieve colorimetric detection of H2O2. A similar H2O2 sensor was reported by Liu et al. [40] by using N-doped C-dots. Shan et al. [41] synthesized boron-doped C-dots as a fluorescent probe for H2O2 detection. Most of the above sensing methods, however, require first synthesis of C-dots followed by surface functionalization which represents elaborate synthesis schemes and relatively long detection times. In addition, since majority of the above sensing modes were based upon quenching of C-dots' fluorescence, the sensitivity and dynamic range are limited by fluorescence detection thresholds.

Here, we report development of a new C-dot-based fluorescence “on/off/on” concept applied for H2O2 sensing. C-dots were prepared from L-penicillamine (LPA) as the carbon precursor. Initially, the fluorescence of the LPA-C-dots was quenched by addition of hemoglobin (Hb), i.e. fluorescence “off” state, induced through π − π interaction between the heme units of Hb and the aromatic ring system of C-dots. However, H2O2 addition fully recovered the LPA-C-dots fluorescence (fluorescence “on”). We show that the dramatic fluorescence recovery effect was likely due to degradation of hemoglobin combined with a unique radical scavenging mechanism affected by the LPA-C-dots. The LPA-C-dot H2O2 sensing scheme is simple, easy to perform, eco-friendly and exhibits good sensitivity.

L-Penicillamine was purchased from Combi-Blocks, USA. Citric acid, hemoglobin, bovine serum albumin (BSA), ferrous sulfate, O,O'-bis(2-amino propyl) polypropylene glycol-block-polyethylene glycol-block-polypropyleneglycol1, potassium bromide, and dialysis tubing (benzoylated, MWCO ∼2000 Da) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich, USA. Hemin was purchased from Sigma Aldrich, China. Hydrogen peroxide was purchased from Alfa Aesar, England. Ethylene glycol was purchased from SDFCL, India. Sodium hydroxide was bought from Frutarom (Haifa, Israel). All chemicals were used without further purification.

200 mg of L-Penicillamine was mixed with 5 mL ethylene glycol and 50 μL of O,O'-bis(2-amino propyl) polypropylene glycol-block-polyethylene glycol-block-polypropylene glycol1. The mixture was heated at ∼140 °C for 3 h for the formation of C-dots. Then the C-dots solution was dialyzed for sample purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of citric acid derived C-dots (CA-C-dots)
ce:section.ce:para: 200 mg citric acid was heated at ∼210 °C until the solid melted completely, and the temperature was held for another 15 min. After cooling to room temperature, 2 mL distilled water was added to it followed by 200 μL of 20 M sodium hydroxide solution. The resulting solution was dialyzed to get rid-of the impurities.

High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) samples were prepared by putting a drop of synthesized C-dots solution on a graphene-coated copper grid followed by drying. Samples were examined with a 200 kV JEOL JEM-2100F microscope. Fluorescence emission spectra were recorded on a FL920 spectrofluorimeter (Edinburgh Instruments, UK). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of C-dots was performed on an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer ESCALAB 250 ultrahigh vacuum (1*10−9 bar) apparatus with an Al K α X-ray source and a monochromator. The X-ray beam size was 500 μm and survey spectra was recorded with pass energy (PE) 150 eV and high energy resolution spectra were recorded with pass energy (PE) 20 eV. Processing of the XPS results was carried out using AVANTGE program. UV–vis absorption spectra were measured using a JASCO V-550 UV–vis spectrophotometer. Circular dichroism spectroscopy was recorded on a Jasco J715 spectropolarimeter (Jasco, Tokyo, Japan) using a quartz 0.1 mm cuvette. Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) of dried samples was recorded on a NICOLET 6700 FTIR spectrometer by preparing pellets with KBr.

Different volumes of hemoglobin (Hb) aqueous solutions (concentration ∼ 310 μM) were mixed with 1.3 mL of each C-dot aqueous solution (C-dots concentration ∼1.0 mg/mL) and incubated for 10 min. The fluorescence of C-dots/Hb complex was measured using an excitation wavelength of 450 nm.

250 μL of 310 μM Hb solution was added with 1.3 mL of each C-dots aqueous solution (C-dots concentration ∼1.0 mg/mL) and incubated for 10 min. Then different amounts of hydrogen peroxide was added to C-dots/Hb complex aqueous solution and incubated for 30 min. Finally fluorescence was measured using 450 nm excitation wavelength.

Fig. 1 depicts the synthesis route and the fluorescence “on/off/on” sensing concept for detection of H2O2. C-dots were synthesized through a single-step carbonization procedure using L-penicillamine (LPA) as the carbon source (Fig. 1A). The as-synthesized LPA-C-dots solution was initially fluorescent (Fig. 1B, “on” state, excitation 365 nm); however, after incubation with hemoglobin (Hb) the fluorescence was quenched (“off” state). Subsequent addition of H2O2 to the C-dot-Hb solution resulted in almost complete recovery of the fluorescence emission (fluorescence “on”). Importantly, a large number (hundreds) on/off/on cycles could be carried out without affecting the fluorescence transformations and C-dot response.

Fig. 2 presents microscopic and spectroscopic characterization of the LPA-generated C-dots. The representative high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) image in Fig. 2A underscores the crystalline graphitic organization of the C-dots' cores, displaying the in-plane lattice spacing of 0.215 nm corresponding to the [110] plane of graphite [46]. Fig. 2B shows the excitation-dependent emission spectra of the LPA-C-dots, which is typical of C-dots. The maximal emission at around 530 nm accounts for the green-yellow color of the LPA-C-dots. The X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) data in Fig. 2C reveal the different functional groups upon the C-dots' surface. Specifically, the deconvoluted C 1 s spectrum displays peaks at 284.8 eV corresponding to sp2 carbon atoms (CC), and at 286.3 eV assigned to COH groups, whereas the deconvoluted O 1 s spectrum shows peaks at 530.9 eV for CO group and 532.6 eV corresponding to OCOH and COH groups (Fig. 2C). In case of nitrogen, the signal at 399.4 eV is assigned to CN and/or CN groups [25–27]. The deconvoluted peaks of S 2p at 161.7 eV, 163.3 eV, and 164.4 eV correspond to the S 2p3/2, S 2p1/2 of SH and SO groups, respectively [47–50]. Importantly, the XPS results indicate that L-penicillamine constituents were retained in the C-dots, as the residues comprising C, O, N and S originate from this precursor [51]. This result is important, since the thrust of the sensing concept demonstrated here is the retention of “structural memory” of the penicillamine carbonaceous precursor in the LPA-C-dots [25,32,34,46].

The first step in the H2O2 sensing strategy demonstrated here was the addition of hemoglobin (Hb) to the LPA-C-dots, resulting in fluorescence quenching (i.e. fluorescence “off”, Fig. 1B). Fig. 3 A demonstrates that the Hb-induced quenching of the C-dots' fluorescence was dependent upon the concentration of added protein. In particular, maximum quenching (∼97%) was observed after incubation of the C-dots with 50 μM Hb (Fig. 3A). Hb-induced quenching of C-dots' fluorescence was previously reported and ascribed to π − π interactions between the heme groups of Hb and aromatic residues comprising the C-dots [29].

Fig. 3B (black curve) shows the dramatic recovery of the Hb-LPA-C-dots' fluorescence following addition of H2O2 (i.e. the “off-on” transformation, Fig. 1B). Specifically, the increase in C-dots' fluorescence was directly related to the concentration of H2O2; almost 100% fluorescence recovery was attained following addition of 100 mM H2O2 (Fig. 3B, black curve). Notably, Fig. 3B indicates a threshold H2O2 detection level of around 10 μM which is low and comparable to other reported H2O2 sensors [38,41,52]. To assess whether the fluorescence recovery was linked to the use of LPA as the C-dot precursor, we also examined the response of Hb-treated C-dots prepared from citric acid (CA) as the carbonaceous building block. The CA-C-dots exhibited similar fluorescence properties (i.e. excitation-dependent emission spectra) as the LPA-C-dots (Fig. S1, SI). Moreover, like the LPA-C-dots, Hb complexation completely quenched the fluorescence of the CA-C-dots (Fig. S2, SI). In contrast, there was no quenching of C-dots fluorescence after addition of BSA (Fig. S3, SI). However, Fig. 3B (red curve) reveals that significantly lower recovery (∼30%) was observed upon addition of H2O2 to the Hb-CA-C-dots, strikingly different than the ∼100% recovery observed upon addition of H2O2 to the Hb-LPA-C-dots complex. This result underscores the significance of LPA-Hb interactions for inducing the fluorescence “off-on” phenomenon.

To account for the mechanistic aspects underlining the fluorescence “on/off/on” phenomenon, we carried out additional spectroscopic analyses (Figs. 4 and 5 ). Fig. 4A presents ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorbance spectra illuminating the effects of Hb binding to the LPA-C-dots, and the impact of subsequent H2O2 addition. Fig. 4A shows that the soret band (410 nm), β-band (541 nm), and α-band (576 nm) of Hb were retained after binding of the protein to the C-dots (green spectrum), although the intensity of the soret band was attenuated compared to free Hb in solution (black spectrum) [53]. This result indicates that Hb maintained its native conformation upon attachment to the LPA-C-dots, even though minor structural perturbations did occur [54]. Addition of H2O2 to Hb-LPA-C-dots, however, completely eliminated the UV absorbance signals of Hb, yielding a featureless spectrum similar to the bare LPA-C-dots (Fig. 4A, blue spectrum).

Circular dichroism (CD) data in Fig. 4B echo the UV–vis analysis. The CD signature of Hb-LPA-C-dot complex (Fig. 4B, green) is almost the same as free Hb (Fig. 4B, black spectrum), featuring the two characteristic negative ellipticities at 208 nm and 222 nm corresponding to the α-helical secondary structure of the protein [55]. However, following addition of H2O2, the α-helical structure of Hb was significantly disrupted leading to a flatter CD trace (Fig. 4B, blue spectrum). Notably, similar attenuation of the CD signal was observed upon addition of H2O2 to free Hb in solution (Fig. 4B, red spectrum), indicating that the structural effects induced by H2O2 upon Hb were similar when the protein was bound to the LPA-C-dots or when it was freely soluble.

To pinpoint the chemical units responsible for the fluorescence quenching/de-quenching phenomena reported here we examined the effects of the heme unit of hemoglobin upon the LPA-C-dots fluorescence (Fig. 5 ). Fig. 5A demonstrates that almost complete quenching of the LPA-C-dots fluorescence was observed upon addition of hemin – a porphyrin ring embedding Fe3+ – at a concentration of 0.2 mM. This quenching was similar to the result recorded in case of Hb-LPA-C-dots (Fig. 3A), indicating that fluorescence quenching of the LPA-C-dots can be traced to interaction of the heme units of hemoglobin with the C-dots' surface. Heme-induced quenching of C-dots' fluorescence has been previously reported and ascribed to efficient energy transfer between C-dots and the porphyrin units [56,57]. We further examined the effects of H2O2 addition upon the fluorescence of the hemin-LPA-C-dot complex and hemin-CA-C-dots (Fig. 5B). Notably, similar to the results obtained in case of Hb-LPA-C-dots and Hb-CA-C-dots (Fig. 3B), Fig. 5B reveals significantly more pronounced fluorescence recovery following H2O2 addition to hemin-LPA-C-dots compared to hemin-CA-C-dots. Overall, the data presented in Fig. 5 points to the heme residue, particularly the porphyrin ring, as the structural unit likely responsible for the on/off/on phenomenon.

Fig. 6 outlines a proposed mechanism for the “on/off/on” H2O2 detection by LPA-C-dots, based upon the experimental data in Figs. 2–5. The C-dot fluorescence is initially quenched upon Hb complexation due to interactions between the heme units of Hb and the C-dots. In particular, quenching is likely induced by energy and electron transfer from the abundant aromatic units in the C-dots (serving as electron donors) to the heme residues of Hb (electron acceptors) [29]. Indeed, the emission spectrum of the C-dots (excited at 450 nm, Fig. 2B) significantly overlapped with the absorption band of Hb (Fig. 4A), potentially enabling photoinduced energy transfer (PET), which might further contribute to quenching of the C-dots' fluorescence. The spectral overlap between the UV–vis absorbance spectrum of Hb and fluorescence emission spectrum of the LPA-C-dots (Fig. S4, SI) reveals a significant overlap, confirming the feasibility of energy transfer [29]. Subsequent addition of H2O2, however, oxidizes and degrades the Hb protein, specifically releasing the C-dot-attached heme residues [31,58,59], thereby recovering the C-dots' fluorescence.

The model in Fig. 6 has been further employed to account for the significant difference between the H2O2-induced fluorescence recovery observed for Hb-LPA-C-dots vs. Hb-CA-C-dots (i.e. Figs. 3, 5). Specifically, as outlined in Fig. 6, Hb exposure to H2O2 gives rise to degradation of the heme moieties and release of iron ions [31,58]. Importantly, oxidation the Fe2+ ions to Fe3+ by H2O2 generates hydroxyl radial ( . OH) and hydroxyl anion (OH−) according to Reaction (1) [60]:
(1) Fe2+ + H2O2 → Fe3+ + OH + OH−

Hydroxyl radicals (OH) have been previously shown to constitute effective quenching agents of C-dot fluorescence [31,58]. Specifically, the high oxidation potential of . OH is believed to disrupt the fluorescence-generating units in the C-dots and consequent fluorescence quenching [57]. Accordingly, interaction and consequent quenching of the CA-C-dots' fluorescence by the hydroxyl radicals generated by Reaction 1 is the likely factor responsible for the incomplete fluorescence recovery observed upon addition of H2O2 to the Hb/CA-C-dot complex (i.e. Fig. 3B).

However, the scenario in case of H2O2 addition to the Hb/LPA-C-dots is significantly different due to the display of the SH groups upon the C-dots’ surface. The presence of thiol units upon the LPA-C-dot surface is confirmed in the XPS and FTIR data (Fig. 2C and Fig. 7 B, below). Notably, the thiol residues can react with the hydroxyl radicals (.OH), producing disulfide bonds, according to the pathway depicted in Fig. 7A [61]. Importantly, scavenging of the hydroxyl radicals by the thiol moieties (Fig. 7A) likely prevents the quenching-inducing interactions of the radicals with the LPA-C-dots, concomitantly giving rise to full recovery of their fluorescence signals, as observed in Figs. 3 and 5. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy measurements examining Hb-LPA-C-dots before and after addition of H2O2, presented in Fig. 7B, lend support to this model. Specifically, FTIR peaks at 690 cm−1 (thiol CS band) and 2580 cm−1 (SH band) [62] were recorded for Hb-LPA-C-dots prior to H2O2 addition (Fig. 7B,i). Following addition of H2O2, these two peaks disappeared (Fig. 7B,ii). In their stead, a vibrational band at 615 cm−1 appeared (Fig. 7B,ii) assigned to CS and SS bands of disulfide [62].

We present a new fluorescence “on-off-on” sensing concept for detection of hydrogen peroxide using C-dots prepared from L-penicillamine as the carbonaceous building block. The LPA-C-dots were coupled to hemoglobin, resulting in significant quenching of the C-dots' fluorescence due to energy transfer and π − π interactions between the aromatic residues of the C-dots and the heme units of Hb. We found, however, that the C-dots' fluorescence could be almost completely recovered upon addition of H2O2 to the Hb-LPA-C-dot complexes. This remarkable phenomenon is likely ascribed to degradation of Hb by H2O2 and subsequent scavenging of hydroxyl radicals generated upon oxidation of the Fe2+ ions. The “on-off-on” sensing concept is simple to carry out and analyze; indeed, sensing based upon dequenching (i.e. “on” fluorescence) is intrinsically more sensitive compared to the overwhelming majority of C-dot-based sensing schemes which rely on fluorescence quenching. Furthermore, synthesis of the LPA-C-dot sensing platform is easy, utilizing inexpensive and “green” building blocks. The sensing concept presented here might be applicable for detection of other oxidants in water. Moreover, the radical scavenging properties of the LPA-C-dots might be further exploited for physiological cell protection and therapeutic applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Dr. Susanta Kumar Bhunia is grateful to the Planning and Budgeting Committee (PBC) of the Israeli Council for Higher Education for an Outstanding Postdoctoral Fellowship. The Israel Ministry of Science and Technology is acknowledged for financial support through a China-Israel collaborative grant no. 23752-3.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.05.029.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

H2O2 determination by the I3-method and by KMnO4 titration
Detection of label-free H 2 O 2 based on sensitive Au nanorods as sensor
A visual detection of hydrogen peroxide on the basis of Fenton reaction with gold nanoparticles
Development of a highly sensitive fluorescence probe for hydrogen peroxide
Fluorescent and luminescent probes for detection of reactive oxygen and nitrogen species
A mitochondria-targeted colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescent probe for hydrogen peroxide with a large emission shift and bio-imaging in living cells
Optical detection of glucose and acetylcholine esterase inhibitors by H2O2-sensitive CdSe/ZnS quantum dots

A carbon dot-hemoglobin complex-based biosensor for cholesterol detection
Carbon-dot-aerogel sensor for aromatic volatile organic compounds

Highly selective detection of phosphate in very complicated matrixes with an off-on fluorescent probe of europium-adjusted carbon dots

Exploiting XPS for the identification of sulfides and polysulfides

Stereoselective interaction between hemoglobin and penicillamine enantiomers modified chiral surfaces

Highly selective fluorescence determination of the hematin level in human erythrocytes with no need for separation from bulk hemoglobin

Radical scavenging efficacy of thiol capped silver nanoparticles
Interpretation of Infrared Spectra, a Practical Approach, Encyclopedia of Analytical Chemistry

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Susanta Kumar Bhunia received his Ph.D. in Chemistry in 2015 from Indian Association for the Cultivation of Science, India, under the supervision of Prof. Nikhil Ranjan Jana. He is currently a postdoctoral research associate with Prof. Lilac Amirav in Schulich Faculty of Chemistry at Technion – Israel Institute of Technology, Haifa, Israel. His research focuses on the development of carbon-based advanced nanostructures for various technological applications, especially nano-biotechnology, sensing and environmental application.

Susmita Dolai received her MS degree in Environmental Chemistry in 2014 from Vidyasagar University, India. She was a project student in 2016-2017 at Ben Gurion University of the Negev, Israel. Currently she is doing her Ph.D. at the Department of Chemistry, Ben Gurion University of the Negev, Beer Sheva, Israel. Her research interest is synthesis of nanomaterials for environmental application.

Prof. Hongchen Sun is a Professor at Jilin Provincial Key Laboratory of Tooth Development and Bone Remodeling, Jilin University, P. R. China. His current research is on construction and biological function of nanoparticles in response to microenvironment, interaction between nanoparticles and subcellular structures, medical microscale diagnosis and treatment.

Prof. Raz Jelinek holds the Carole and Barry Kaye Chair in Applied Science at the Department of Chemistry, Ben Gurion University, and is also a founding member of the Ilse Katz Institute for Nanoscale Science and Technology (IKI). Prof. Jelinek’s research is broad-based, and he has worked in varied fields including “bottom-up” chemistry, bioand chemo-sensors, amyloid peptides, membranes, and carbon nanoparticles.
